does: This paper reframes governance as an engineering discipline and introduces a formal framework inspired by metamaterials physics to model institutional coordination under AGI-driven acceleration. The core contribution is a phenomenological constitutive law quantifying effective institutional responsiveness as a function of decision branching factor, provenance fidelity, verification rate, and correlated-detection synergy. The framework predicts a sharp phase transition between self-healing and catastrophic regimes, identifying what the authors call the Freezing Equilibrium as a stable Nash equilibrium where rational actors default to inaction when AI output validation costs exceed expected utility. The mathematical model provides quantitative predictions about when coordination mechanisms succeed or fail as decision velocity increases.
connects: This work extends governance and coordination theory by incorporating insights from materials physics and phase transitions, responding to recent concerns about AI systems outpacing human verification capacity. It builds on prior research in institutional design, mechanism design, and technological governance by formalizing intuitions about coordination failures under capability mismatches. The paper engages with frameworks treating governance as a design problem rather than purely normative enterprise, and connects to literature on AI safety and verification bottlenecks that highlight fundamental limits to human oversight. The metamaterials analogy draws from condensed matter physics to provide structural scaffolding for understanding how microstructural properties of institutions generate macroscopic coordination behavior, extending methodological borrowing from physics into social systems.
enables: The framework enables quantitative analysis of institutional design choices and suggests specific interventions to prevent catastrophic coordination failure. By identifying which parameters most control phase transitions, the model points toward designing governance structures with sufficient provenance fidelity and verification capacity to maintain self-healing operation even as decision velocity increases. Future work could empirically calibrate the model using historical institutional data, extend it to heterogeneous agent populations with varying capabilities, or develop control strategies that adaptively adjust parameters as capability gradients widen. The formalism also enables exploration of novel institutional structures inspired by metamaterial design principles, potentially revealing non-obvious coordination solutions and informing policy responses to advanced AI deployment.
